sectionName: Additional deduction for interest on home loan for affordable housing (loans sanctioned April 2019 – March 2022)
description: Section 80EEA provides an additional deduction of up to ₹1,50,000 per year on interest paid on home loans for affordable housing, over and above the ₹2,00,000 limit under Section 24(b). This applies to loans sanctioned between April 1, 2019 and March 31, 2022 for properties with a stamp duty value of ₹45 lakh or less. The sanction window is closed; existing qualifying borrowers continue to benefit. Maximum combined interest deduction with Section 24(b) is ₹3,50,000 per year.
eligibleAssessees: Resident Individual (first-time home buyer with qualifying loan)
notEligible: Individuals who already owned any residential property on the date of loan sanction
Individuals whose loan was sanctioned before April 1, 2019 or after March 31, 2022
Taxpayers also claiming Section 80EE for the same property
Taxpayers opting for new tax regime (Section 115BAC)

eligibleInvestments.name: Interest on affordable housing loan — additional deduction over and above Section 24(b)
eligibleInvestments.notes: Loan must be sanctioned between April 1, 2019 and March 31, 2022. Stamp duty value of the residential property must be ₹45 lakh or less (note: this is the stamp duty value, not the market/agreement value). Borrower must not have owned any other residential house property on the loan sanction date. Cannot simultaneously claim Section 80EE for the same or different property.

conditions: Available only under the old tax regime.
Loan must have been sanctioned by a financial institution between April 1, 2019 and March 31, 2022 — the window is closed for new loans.
The stamp duty value (government circle rate value) of the residential property must be ₹45,00,000 or less at the time of sanction — not the market value or agreement value.
The taxpayer must be a first-time home buyer — no residential house property owned on the date of loan sanction.
An individual cannot claim both Section 80EE and Section 80EEA — they are mutually exclusive.
Deduction is available each year until the loan is fully repaid.
The loan must be taken from a financial institution (bank, housing finance company, NBFC) — not from private individuals.
The property should be residential in nature.
documentationRequired: Loan sanction letter from bank/housing finance company (must show sanction date between April 1, 2019 and March 31, 2022)
Annual interest certificate from lender for FY 2025-26
Stamp duty valuation document or registration document showing stamp duty value of property
Self-declaration that no other residential house property was owned on the date of loan sanction

keyRules.rule: 80EEA is a sunset provision for affordable housing — no new loans sanctioned after March 31, 2022 qualify. Existing borrowers with loans sanctioned between April 1, 2019 and March 31, 2022 continue to benefit for the remaining loan tenure.
keyRules.legalReference: Section 80EEA — condition on loan sanction period

keyRules.rule: The property value test under 80EEA uses STAMP DUTY VALUE (government circle rate), NOT the agreement value or market price. If stamp duty value is ₹45 lakh or below, the condition is met even if the actual market price is higher.
Maximum combined interest deduction for a qualifying 80EEA borrower: ₹2,00,000 (Section 24(b)) + ₹1,50,000 (80EEA) = ₹3,50,000 per year on the same property.

80EE and 80EEA are mutually exclusive. A taxpayer who qualifies for 80EE (loan sanctioned in 2016-17) cannot also claim 80EEA, and vice versa. However, in practice, the different sanction date windows make simultaneous eligibility impossible for the same loan.
RERA registration of the property is NOT mandatorily required for 80EEA, but the property must be residential. Commercial properties do not qualify.

commonMistakes: Confusing 80EEA with 80EE — 80EEA is for loans sanctioned April 2019 to March 2022 (affordable housing ₹45L stamp duty); 80EE is for loans sanctioned April 2016 to March 2017 (₹50L property, ₹35L loan). They are mutually exclusive.
Using market value or agreement value instead of stamp duty value to check the ₹45 lakh threshold — the condition is on stamp duty value.
Claiming 80EEA for a loan sanctioned after March 31, 2022 — no new loans qualify.
Not continuing to claim 80EEA in subsequent years on an existing qualifying loan — the benefit persists until full repayment.
Claiming 80EEA without confirming first-time buyer status — if the taxpayer owned any residential property on the loan sanction date, they are ineligible.
Forgetting that 80EEA is over and above Section 24(b) — some taxpayers only claim one and miss the other.
Claiming 80EEA in the new tax regime — it is only available under the old regime.
relatedSections: Section 24(b) — Interest on home loan (₹2,00,000 per year for self-occupied; 80EEA is additional beyond this)
Section 80EE — Additional interest deduction for FY 2016-17 loans (mutually exclusive with 80EEA)
Section 80C — Principal repayment of home loan
Section 115BAC — New tax regime (80EEA not available)

workedExample.profile: First-time buyer. Loan of ₹38 lakh sanctioned June 2020 for a flat with stamp duty value ₹42 lakh. Annual interest in FY 2025-26: ₹3,20,000. Section 24(b): ₹2,00,000 (capped). Section 80EEA: ₹1,20,000 (remaining ₹1,20,000 of actual interest, within ₹1,50,000 limit).
workedExample.taxSaving: 80EEA deduction ₹1,20,000 (actual interest remaining after 24(b) cap). At 30% slab: ₹36,000 + 4% cess = ₹37,440 additional tax saved. Total interest relief on this property: ₹2,00,000 (24(b)) + ₹1,20,000 (80EEA) = ₹3,20,000.
